speaker: host
text: You rarely mention your brother unless risk gets personal.

speaker: guest
text: I usually keep that part off stage because it belongs to him too.

speaker: host
text: The tension shows up again when you describe the Nevada restructuring.

speaker: guest
text: That period forced every decision into a human cost ledger.

speaker: host
text: You also reversed yourself on whether a public company could protect the mission.

speaker: guest
text: I changed my mind when scale started looking like a shield instead of theater.

speaker: host
text: Go back to the factory town for a minute because that still frames your language.

speaker: guest
text: When the mill died people stopped trusting anyone who sounded abstract.

speaker: host
text: That sounds close to how you talk about your brother's relapse.

speaker: guest
text: It is close because after that I stopped pretending risk was only strategic.

speaker: host
text: Was there one conversation with him that changed your leadership more than the rest?

speaker: guest
text: He asked why I could model downside for factories but not for family.

speaker: host
text: That also makes the layoffs sound less like an operations story.

speaker: guest
text: Right, because the hardest part was knowing people heard silence where they needed candor.

speaker: host
text: So the family thread and the labor thread are actually the same trust problem.

speaker: guest
text: That is the connection I resisted saying out loud for years.
